coredata.dc:title: Bi2MoO6 nanoflower-like microsphere photocatalyst modified by boron doped carbon quantum dots: Improving the photocatalytic degradation performance of BPA in all directions
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xinxin
Chen, Changzhao
Zang, Jiyuan
coredata.dc:description: Bi-based semiconductor photocatalyst represented by Bi2MoO6 (BMO) has obvious advantages in the degradation of bisphenol A (BPA), but three prominent problems that restrict the improvement of photocatalytic performance, namely, insufficient solar absorption, less active sites and serious photogenerated carrier recombination, need to be solved urgently. For this reason, we specially designed a simple structure of B-doped carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) modified BMO nanoflower-like microsphere composite to improve the photocatalytic performance of the system from the above three dimensions. The optimized BCQDs/BMO sample completely degraded BPA within 120 min under simulated sunlight, corresponding to an apparent constant of 0.03453 min−1, while the control sample of CQDs/BMO without B doping only degraded about 84 % of BPA within 160 min, with an apparent coefficient of 0.01138 min−1. Even after filtering out UV components below 400 nm, the degradation performance of the optimal composite catalyst did not decrease. The improved catalytic performance comes from the excellent up-conversion luminescence performance and good electron storage capacity of CQDs on the one hand, and the introduction of B on the other hand can adjust the surface structure of CQDs to generate more active sites and functional interfaces to further promote the catalytic reaction. Free radical trapping and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests have shown that·OH and·O2 - are the main active substances in the degradation of BPA. The current BCQDs/BMO catalysts, despite their simple structure, can simultaneously improve the catalytic performance from the three important aspects mentioned above, which will guide the design of others semiconductor based photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Bi2MoO6 nanoflower-like microsphere photocatalyst modified by boron doped carbon quantum dots: Improving the photocatalytic degradation performance of BPA in all directions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bi-based semiconductor photocatalyst represented by Bi2MoO6 (BMO) has obvious advantages in the degradation of bisphenol A (BPA), but three prominent problems that restrict the improvement of photocatalytic performance, namely, insufficient solar absorption, less active sites and serious photogenerated carrier recombination, need to be solved urgently. For this reason, we specially designed a simple structure of B-doped carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) modified BMO nanoflower-like microsphere composite to improve the photocatalytic performance of the system from the above three dimensions. The optimized BCQDs/BMO sample completely degraded BPA within 120 min under simulated sunlight, corresponding to an apparent constant of 0.03453 min−1, while the control sample of CQDs/BMO without B doping only degraded about 84 % of BPA within 160 min, with an apparent coefficient of 0.01138 min−1. Even after filtering out UV components below 400 nm, the degradation performance of the optimal composite catalyst did not decrease. The improved catalytic performance comes from the excellent up-conversion luminescence performance and good electron storage capacity of CQDs on the one hand, and the introduction of B on the other hand can adjust the surface structure of CQDs to generate more active sites and functional interfaces to further promote the catalytic reaction. Free radical trapping and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests have shown that·OH and·O2 - are the main active substances in the degradation of BPA. The current BCQDs/BMO catalysts, despite their simple structure, can simultaneously improve the catalytic performance from the three important aspects mentioned above, which will guide the design of others semiconductor based photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, plastic products are ubiquitous and widely used in people's daily life and industrial production, but with them comes the serious problem of plastic pollution [1–3]. Bisphenol A (BPA) is used industrially to synthesize materials such as polycarbonate and epoxy resins, and has been used since the 1960s to make plastic (milk) bottles, sippy cups for young children, and coatings on the inside of food and beverage (powdered milk) cans [4–6]. BPA is everywhere, from mineral water bottles, medical devices, and the inside of food packaging. Every year, tens of millions of tons of BPA-containing plastics are produced worldwide. However, if exposure time is too prolonged or the exposure is too high, it may lead to endocrine disorders, interfering with the reproductive system and inducing precocious puberty in children [7,8]. Therefore, utilizing a feasible means to degrade BPA is of great significance. In this regard, methods such as physical adsorption [9], biodegradation [10], and ion exchange [11] are widely used for the removal of pollutants from wastewater. However, all of these methods have some drawbacks, such as small adsorption capacity and easy saturation during use, low productivity as well as relatively harsh degradation conditions. Photocatalytic technology is one of the most active research fields in recent years, which has great advantages in pollutant degradation and wastewater treatment. Due to its simple process, low energy consumption, easy control of operating conditions and easy availability of photocatalytic materials, thorough material degradation, and no secondary pollution, it is considered a new environmental protection technology with good development prospects.

Bi2MoO6 (BMO) was often used to degrade BPA effectively due to its unique structure, suitable bandgap, and abundant crystalline morphology [12,13]. As one of the simplest Aurivillius-type oxides, BMO is composed of alternating stacks of (Bi2O2)2+ layers and co-angular MoO4 2- calcite sheets, and the Bi-O layer is an octahedral shared perovskite-like structure [14,15]. This structure can provide sufficient space for electron delocalization and promote catalytic reactions. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of BMO are composed of Bi 6s and 6p orbitals [16–18], respectively, in which Bi 6s orbitals can promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole (e--h+) pairs [19], making the bandgap of BMO narrower (2.5–2.8 eV) [20,21], and the light absorption range extends to the visible light region, overcoming the defect of TiO2 having only ultraviolet light absorption ability. This is an important reason why BMO has high photocatalytic activity. In addition, the rich crystal form also provides the possibility for modulating the morphology of BMO for photocatalytic research. The common crystal forms are hollow nanospheres [22,23], nanoflower-like microspheres [24], nanobelts [25], nanoflakes [26], nano bird nests [27] and nanoparticles [28]. Among them, nanoflower-like microspheres are particularly effective in improving photocatalytic activity because of their fully exposed surfaces [29,30]. Although the photocatalytic activity of BMO photocatalyst in the visible light region has significantly improved compared to TiO2, its quantum efficiency is still not high, and there is still a long distance from practical application.

In fact, single BMO nanomaterials have three prominent shortcomings: fewer active sites, lower utilization of visible light, and serious carrier recombination [31–33]. Therefore, BMO photocatalysts mainly focus on the above issues for research. Common strategies include doping [34], morphology control [35], metal loading [36], and construction of heterogeneous or composite structures [37]. In this regard, B-doped carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) decorated onto nanoflower-like BMO microspheres is a better choice. Firstly, the unique up-conversion photoluminescence properties of CQDs allow them to absorb visible light and convert it to ultraviolet or shorter wavelengths, thus maximizing the use of sunlight [38]. Secondly, CQDs has good electron storage capacity and can exhibit synergistic effects in the catalyst, forming spatial separation centers for photogenerated charge carriers and effectively promoting the separation of photogenerated e--h+ pairs [39]. Thirdly, B doping is used to purposefully modify CQDs. By introducing non-metallic atoms (e.g., N, S, P, B) into CQDs, its photoelectronic properties can be tuned, and different elements have their unique advantages in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CQDs. For example, N-doped CQDs can produce a strong electron absorption effect, leading to a blue shift in the photoluminescence spectrum [40], S-doped CQDs improve the electrical conductivity of the material [41], and P-doped CQDs promote the e- transfer rate and reduce the carrier recombination rate [42]. Due to the difference of electronegativity and valence state between B and C atoms, B doping of CQDs can adjust the charge density and spin density of carbon atoms in CQDs, providing more active sites [43,44]. In addition, doping with B atoms can introduce rich functional groups into CQDs, constructing functional interfaces that are easy to bind with BMO, which is more conducive to the entry of e- from BMO into CQDs. Finally, the BMO nanoflower shaped microspheres designed by us are made by combining extremely thin BMO nanoflakes twisted into petals, while BCQDs' nanoparticles are dotted on the petals. The maximum exposure of BMO surface means that more BCQDs can be loaded on it, thus providing more active sites. Therefore, BCQDs modified BMO catalyst uses its own simple structural design to achieve the key factors such as the increase of active sites, the enhancement of visible light absorption and the inhibition of carrier recombination, which will guide the design of others semiconductor based photocatalysts.

In this work, we systematically carried out an experimental study on the degradation of BPA by BCQDs modified Bi2MoO6 nanoflower photocatalyst. Thanks to the up-conversion and electron storage properties of CQDs, the catalytic performance of CQDs modified BMO (whether B doped or not) has been significantly improved compared to a single BMO. Importantly, the introduction of B element into CQDs will further enhance the degradation efficiency of BPA. The optimized BCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite catalyst completely degraded BPA under simulated light for about 120 min, while the control sample CQDs/Bi2MoO6 showed only 84 % degradation in 160 min. The further improvement of degradation performance is mainly attributed to the successful doping of B element, which to some extent regulates the surface structure of CQDs, leading to more active sites and functional interfaces that promote catalytic reaction. Importantly, the band edge positions of BCQDs/BMO and CQDs/BMO at the same ratio and the main active substances for the degradation of BPA were analyzed by Mott Schottky, radical trapping experiments and electron spin resonance tests. The doping of B elements promoted the interfacial coupling and modulated the energy band structure of the composites, which was favorable to the generation of active radicals.

The drugs used were boric acid (H3BO3), citric acid (C6H8O7), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2), benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) and ammonium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O). The reagent chosen for the degradation test was bisphenol A (BPA). All the pharmaceuticals were obtained from Shanghai Maclean Chemical Reagent Company and were not further purified.

Preparation of B-doped CQDs (BCQDs): C6H8O7 as the carbon precursor and H3BO3 as the boron source. The specific steps were as follows: 0.9606 g C6H8O7 was mixed with 0.371 g H3BO3, 335 μL ethylenediamine and 10 mL deionized water were added, sonicated and stirred for 30 min each, followed by hydrothermal heating at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After allowing to come to room temperature, the brown product was placed in a centrifuge at 8500 rpm for 8 min, and finally dialyzed by deionized water with MD44–3500 osmosis membrane for 24 h, during which the water was changed at an interval of 6 h. Then the solution was put into a freeze-dryer and the freezing time was set to 2 h and the drying time to 24 h to obtain BCQDs. Under the same conditions, CQDs without element B were synthesized using C6H8O7.

Preparation of Bi
2
MoO
6
microspheres: 0.477 g Bi(NO3)3 and 0.119 g Na2MoO4 were dissolved in 10 mL ethylene glycol, mixed and sonicated for 30 min, stirred for 1 h. Add 60 mL of anhydrous ethanol to above solution. Hydrothermally treated at 160 ℃ for 24 h, washed 3 times alternately with water and ethanol each, and finally dried in an oven at 80 ℃, and recorded as BMO.

Preparation of BC6/BMO: 0.477 g Bi(NO3)3 and 0.119 g Na2MoO4 were dissolved in 10 mL ethylene glycol, mixed and sonicated for 30 min, 6 mg BCQDs was added to the above solution, and then 60 mL anhydrous ethanol was added. The remaining steps were the same as in the above experiments, and final product was recorded as BC6/BMO. Similarly, the composite C6/BMO without B-doped elements was synthesized using CQDs.

The catalytic performance of the samples was evaluated by monitoring the degradation of BPA (20 mg/L, 60 mL) solution under simulated light. The catalyst was administered at 20 mg and the mixed solution was sonicated for 7 min and then magnetically stirred in dark environment for 60 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp is used as a simulated sunlight source. Four milliliters of the suspension were removed from the beaker every 20 min. Turn on the UV-Vis spectrophotometer, quartz cuvettes were filled with deionized water, swept the baseline, and then put the supernatant in turn for testing. Observe the measured full spectrum with or without spurious peaks, and record the Abs value of each sample for further analysis.

The XRD patterns of BMO, BC/BMO hybrids with different composite ratio and the control sample of C6/BMO are shown in Fig. 1. BMO presents six major diffraction peaks located at 28.14°, 32.46°, 36.97°, 46.66°, 55.45° and 58.53°, respectively, corresponding to the (131), (002), (151), (202), (331) and (262) crystal planes of the BMO (JCPDS card no. 76-2388) [45]. No peaks of CQDs were found in the XRD spectra, and there was no significant change in the diffraction peak of BMO, indicating that the amount of BCQDs in the composite sample was relatively small, and its introduction did not affect the crystal structure of BMO.

As seen in the SEM images, the synthesized BMO showed a unique flower-like microsphere structure with a diameter of about 1.8 µm ( Fig. 2(a)), which was actually self-assembled from two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets with a thickness of 20–30 nm (Fig. 2(b)). This flower-like structure ensures maximum adhesion of BCQDs, allowing them to intersperse uniformly on the BMO nano petals. Due to its larger exposure area compared to other forms of nanostructures (nanosheets, nanopillars, etc.), it is more conducive to photocatalytic reactions. The HRTEM image of BC6/BMO were analyzed to further reveal the fine structure of the composite (Fig. 2(c)). The lattice spacings of 0.318 nm and 0.275 nm belong to the (131) and (002) crystal planes of BMO [46,47], while
d = 0.334 nm
is the plane spacing of BCQDs (002) facet [48]. In addition, Mo, O, Bi, C, and B signals can be clearly observed in the EDS mapping of BC6/BMO (Fig. 2(d-h)). Due to the absence of B and C elements in BMO, B and C in the composite sample must come from the prepared BCQDs.

To further analyze the structure of the prepared samples and to identify their surface functional groups, FTIR tests were performed on the samples. The FTIR spectra of BCQDs and CQDs are very similar ( Fig. 3(a)). The peaks at 1048, 1645 and 3378 cm−1 belong to C-O, CO and O-H stretching vibration [44,49], respectively, from which the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs can be clearly judged, and the abundant oxygen-containing groups promote the hydrophilicity and stability of CQDs. It should be noted that after B doping, the absorption peak intensity of the CO bond significantly weakens, indicating that the introduction of B element disturbs the hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CQDs [43], which may be the most important difference in the absorption spectra of CQDs and BCQDs. In addition, three weaker peaks can be observed, corresponding to B-O stretching (1358 cm−1), B-O-H bending (1190 cm−1) and C-B stretching vibrations (1103 cm−1) [50,51]. The formation of these B bonds indicates that B doping can introduce rich functional groups into CQDs, which is beneficial for constructing functional interfaces that bind to BMO, thereby promoting the smooth entry of electrons from BMO into CQDs. Pure BMO has strong absorption in the range of 500–1000 cm−1 (Fig. 3(b)), which is ascribed to Bi-O, Mo-O stretching modes and deformation vibrations [52]. The absorption peak of the composite material is mainly dominated by BMO, but in order to observe the variation of the FTIR spectrum of the composite sample more clearly, we zoomed in on the shaded part of the figure for further analysis. The results are shown in Fig. 3(c), and for the BC/BMO or C/BMO samples, two new peaks appear in the 1400 cm−1-1800 cm−1 band, where the -COO- absorption peak at 1402 cm−1 and the CO vibrational absorption band at 1703 cm−1 originates from the BCQDs (or CQDs) [48]. The peaks gradually become obvious as the doping of BCQDs increases. Although its intensity is weak, it is evidence of electronic coupling at the interface.

The surface composition and the chemical bond of surface atoms of BC6/BMO were analyzed by XPS to further reveal the influence of B doping on the electronic structure of the interface. As expected, Bi, Mo, O, C, and B elements were observed in the XPS full spectrum of the BC6/BMO sample ( Fig. 4(a)). In the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 4(b)), the peak at 158.9 eV for the BMO sample belongs to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 has a higher binding energy at 164.1 eV, indicating Bi specie is in trivalent ionic state [53]. The Mo 3d peak (Fig. 4(c)) can be convolved into two peaks at 232.0 and 235.2 eV, belonging to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo6+ [54]. Two distinct peaks appear in the O 1 s spectrum of BMO (Fig. 4(d)), which corresponds well to Bi-O at 529.7 eV and Mo-O at 530.9 eV [55]. After loading BCQDs on BMO, a significant shift of the Bi 4f, Mo 3d and O 1 s peaks of BC6/BMO can be found. Compared with pure BMO, Bi 4f7/2 and Mo 3d3/2 move slightly in the direction of low binding energy, while O 1 s are shifted in the opposite direction. The binding energy of anions and cations in BMO shifted in opposite directions, respectively, indicating that interfacial coupling played an important role. The C 1s signal peaks of BC6/BMO (Fig. 4(e)) are mainly from the C-C (284.6 eV), C-O (285.9 eV) and CO (287.8 eV) [56,57]. The strengths of C-O bonds and CO bonds from BCQDs in BC6/BMO was significantly reduced as compared with the control sample of C6/BMO, indicating that the introduction of B perturbed the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs, which was consistent with the FTIR results. The large electronegativity of B atom (B=2.04, C=2.55) and electron deficiency make it difficult to dope B atoms into the framework of CQDs, which will inevitably lead to a small amount of doping of B atoms in BCQDs [58]. For this reason, B is difficult to be detected by XPS after recombination with BMO. Therefore, XPS measurement and analysis of element B are focused on BCQDs rather than BC6/BMO. BCQDs contain 4 elements, namely B, C, N and O (inset in Fig. 4(f)). In the B 1s spectrum, the fitted peaks at 185.0 and 188.7 eV consisted mainly of B-C bonds and B-O bonds [44,59], indicating the presence of B elements in the BCQDs. The combined HRTEM, EDS mapping, FTIR and XPS analyses demonstrated the successful preparation of BC6/BMO samples.

The light absorption edge of pure BMO is around 480 nm ( Fig. 5(a)), which exhibits a significant red shift after combination with BCQDs. Further, the absorption curves of all composite samples in the visible light band are significantly elevated compared to pure BMO, indicating enhanced visible light absorption. This is owing to the unique up-conversion photoluminescence of CQDs. The inset of Fig. 5(a) gives the UV–vis absorption curve of BCQDs, which shows that BCQDs have strong light absorption in both the UV and vis regions. A strong typical absorption peak observed at 210 nm is the result of the π-π * jump of the aromatic sp2 structural domain, while the two weak peaks at 330 nm and 374 nm correspond to the n-π * leaps in the CO band [60]. In addition, the aqueous solution of BCQDs showed brown color under natural light and intense bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, reflecting the down-conversion photoluminescence property of BCQDs. The band gaps for BMO and BC6/BMO are 2.74 and 2.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(b)). Therefore, the reduction of band gap and significant enhancement of visible light absorption in composite samples are important factors for improving their photocatalytic performance.

For semiconductor photocatalysts, adsorption plays a crucial role, which is manifested as a complex interaction of competition or collaboration between adsorption and photocatalysis [9,10,61]. In order to eliminate the influence of adsorption effect on the concentration of degraded substances, an adsorption equilibrium experiment was conducted before the photocatalytic experiment. As shown in Fig. 6(a), samples were collected for BPA concentration testing at 30 and 60 min of adsorption, and it was found that the adsorption rates of the two were the same, indicating that the adsorption had reached equilibrium when treated in a dark room for 60 min. Fig. 6(b) shows the variation of BPA degradation rate of the samples after deducting adsorption with irradiation time under simulated sunlight, where the degradation properties of the composites were all improved compared to pure BMO. Previous studies have shown that CQDs not only have excellent up-conversion or down-conversion photoluminescence properties, but also can act as “electron reservoir” [56]. When light is irradiated onto CQDs/BMO, CQDs of up-conversion performance can convert near-infrared light to shorter wavelengths to excites BMO to produce e- and h+. In addition, the e- on the CB of BMO can be trapped by CQDs, thus reducing the complexation rate of photogenerated e--h+ pairs. In this experiment, the photocatalytic efficiency was obviously further improved by replacing pure CQDs with BCQDs. The degradation efficiency on BPA by the control sample of C6/BMO was only 84 % within 160 min, while BC6/BMO degraded up to completely degradation in two hours. The Langmiur-Hinshelwood model was fitted to the degradation data using the integration method, and the best correlation coefficient was obtained after the first level fitting, and the results are shown in Fig. 6(c). Linear variations of
Ln (
c
c
0
)
∼t curves indicated that the degradation of BPA solution was in accordance with the first level reaction kinetics law. The kinetic constant k of BC6/BMO (0.03453 min−1) was about 3 times that of the control sample of C6/BMO (0.01138 min−1), indicating that the introduction of B contributed to the degradation efficiency of the composites. Since the band gap of BMO is located in the visible light range, it is suitable as a photocatalytic material for the degradation of pollutants under visible light conditions. Therefore, a filter with a cutoff wavelength of 400 nm was loaded to measure the degradation performance of the current catalyst under visible light and compare it with the photocatalytic performance under direct irradiation of xenon lamps. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the visible light photocatalytic performance of the composite sample is still superior to that of the individual BMO. The degradation rate of BMO decreased from 46 % under simulated sunlight to 26 % under visible light irradiation, while the degradation rate of the composite sample was above 40 % under visible light. It is worth mentioning that the optimized BC6/BMO has almost no change in the degradation rate under two lighting conditions, and can still completely degrade BPA within 160 min, indicating its very robust visible light catalytic activity.

Furthermore, the reusability of the catalyst was tested through cyclic experiments. In photocatalytic experiments the photocatalyst is collected by centrifugation [62], washed five times alternately with deionized water and ethanol and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The dried photocatalyst can be used directly for the next photocatalytic experiment without further processing. To compensate for the reduction in catalyst mass during centrifugation, two sets of cycling experiments were carried out simultaneously, one of which was supplemented to ensure that the catalyst mass remained constant before each cycle. The degradation efficiency of BC6/BMO for BPA was still as high as 96 % after four cycles ( Fig. 7), implying that the sample has high photocatalytic efficiency and stability for practical applications.

The comparative study of different BMO-based semiconductor photocatalysts for the degradation of BPA is listed in Table 1. Sensitivity and energy efficiency are key factors for evaluating photocatalysts, so the performance was compared in terms of several important indicators such as catalyst dosage, reaction time and degradation rate. Taking into account variables such as dye concentration, catalyst input, degradation time and rate, the designed BCQDs/BMO composite catalyst has great advantages in degrading BPA, achieving 100 % degradation in a relatively short period of time with fewer doses.

In order to reveal the effect of specific surface area (SSA) on the photocatalytic performance of the system, we conducted experimental studies on N2 adsorption and desorption. Fig. 8 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the BC6/BMO and C6/BMO samples with the inset showing the distribution of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size, from which information on SSA and porosity can be obtained, respectively. Both samples show IV isotherms and mesoporous pore size distributions [72], showing the SSAs of 46.2683 and 42.3960 m2/g and the pore volumes of 0.2608 and 0.1977 cm3/g, respectively corresponding to BC6/BMO and C6/BMO ( Table 2). Obviously, the SSA of the BC6/BMO sample increased by 10 % compared to the control sample, which is beneficial for the catalytic reaction. More importantly, B doping not only increases the SSA, but also creates rich functional interfaces, providing more active sites to fully contact and adsorb more active substances, thus improving photocatalytic efficiency.

In addition to the improving visible light utilization and increasing the number of active sites, another consideration of the current BCQDs/BMO catalyst design is the suppression of the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. The macroscopic measurement methods of transient photocurrent response, EIS, and PL spectroscopy can indirectly characterize the carrier dynamics behavior. The transient photocurrent response of BMO, BC6/BMO and C6/BMO was tested using a xenon lamp as a simulated light source ( Fig. 9(a)) and it can be seen that the photocurrent of BC6/BMO (0.6861 μA/cm2) is significantly higher than that of C6/BMO (0.3791 μA/cm2). After filtering out wavelength components below 400 nm, the photocurrent intensity significantly decreases, which is caused by a sharp decrease in the number of photo-generated carriers per unit time. The photocurrent intensity of BMO decreased from 0.1467 A/cm2 to 0.0211 A/cm2, while BC6/BMO still maintained the optimal photocurrent response (Fig. 9(b)). In addition, its Nyquist radius in EIS measurements is smaller than that of C6/BMO (Fig. 9(c)), indicating that B doping increases the functionalized surface structural defects and improves the extraction of h+, which promotes efficient carrier migration and reduces the e--h+ recombination rate [73]. Fig. 9(d) shows the PL spectra of the prepared samples, with an excitation wavelength of 343 nm. The luminescence peak centered around 430 nm originates from the recombination of photo-generated e- and h+ in BMO, which is significantly reduced for BCQDs/BMO composite as compared with BMO and the control sample of C6/BMO. This indicates that the doping of B plays a crucial role in suppressing carrier recombination.

The in-depth analysis and research of degradation mechanisms require precise determination of the energy level positions of composite materials and an understanding of the active species involved in photocatalytic degradation. The positions of the CB and VB of semiconductor photocatalytic materials determine the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts in photooxidation and photoreduction. To this end, MS testing was used to determine the band edge positions of BMO, BC6/BMO, and C6/BMO with respect to analyze the effect of B doping on band positions. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the MS curve of BMO exhibits a positive slope, indicating that BMO is an n-type semiconductor. For n-type semiconductors, the CB is generally considered to be approximately equal to its flat-band potential [74], therefore, the CB potential of BMO is − 0.89 eV vs. RHE. Similarly, the CB potentials of BC6/BMO and C6/BMO can be derived as − 0.61 eV vs. RHE and − 0.75 eV vs. RHE (Fig. 10 (b-c)). Combined with the bandgap data, the VB potentials of BMO, BC6/BMO and C6/BMO are 1.85, 2.03 and 1.75 eV vs. RHE, respectively, using the equation
E
VB
=
E
g
+
E
CB
.

The main active substances of BC6/BMO in the degradation of BPA were analyzed using BQ and IPA as trapping agents to scavenge superoxide (·O2 -) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) ( Fig. 11). The results showed that the BQ greatly reduced the degradation efficiency of BPA, verifying that·O2 - are the main active substances in the degradation process of BPA. Although the addition of IPA also weakened the degradation performance of BC6/BMO, the inhibitory effect was significantly weaker than that of BQ, indicating that·O2 - are the most essential free radicals in the degradation of BPA.

ESR testing was used to further verify the generation and role of superoxide and hydroxyl groups in the degradation process of BPA. As seen in Fig. 12 (a), ESR signals of DMPO-·O2 - for both BC6/BMO and C6/BMO under dark conditions were all zeros, indicating that there were no·O2 - in the catalyst solution under no lighting conditions. Immediately after irradiation, the presence of DMPO-·O2 - ESR signal in the solution was detected, and the signal intensity of BC6/BMO sample significantly exceeded C6/BMO, indicating that BC6/BMO has a higher·O2 - generation rate. Similarly, a characteristic peak of DMPO-·OH with a relative intensity of 1:2:2:1 was captured in BC6/BMO (Fig. 12 (b)), demonstrating that the composite is capable of producing·OH under light conditions, but the ESR intensity of·OH is significantly weaker than that of·O2 - (Fig. 12 (c)). In contrast, the ESR signal of DMPO-·OH under light conditions for C6/BMO was almost invisible (Fig. 12 (d)), indicating the absence of·OH free radicals, which was caused by its VB position being not positive enough. Therefore,·O2 - is the key free radical in the process of photodegradation, which is consistent with the free radical capture experiment.

As can be seen from the energy level diagrams of BC6/BMO and the control sample C6/BMO ( Fig. 13), both samples have a more negative CB potential than the O2/·O2 - potential (−0.33 eV vs. RHE), indicating that they are both capable of reducing O2 to form ·O2 -. However, the CB potential of BC6/BMO (−0.61 eV) was closer to E(O2/·O2 -) than that of the C6/BMO sample, which was more favorable for the generation of ·O2 - involved in BPA degradation [75]. In addition, the VB potential of C6/BMO (1.75 eV) is more negative than that of E(OH-/·OH) (1.99 eV vs. RHE), indicating that h+ in C6/BMO cannot oxidize OH- to produce ·OH. Notably, the VB potential of BC6/BMO is 2.03 eV, which is slightly more positive than E(OH-/·OH), indicating that BC6/BMO can generate ·OH in the photocatalytic process and generate ·O2 more easily than ·OH, which has been confirmed in the ESR test. The above analysis shows that B element doping modulates the energy band structure of the composites to a certain extent and promotes the production of active substances.

Based on the above analysis, we propose a possible mechanism for BPA degradation. Under simulated solar radiation, the sufficient absorption of sunlight by BC6/BMO can generate a large number of e--h+ pairs. The BCQDs dotted on the surface of BMO has the dual effects of absorbing electrons and providing active sites, which makes e- quickly migrate from the interior of semiconductor to the surface and be absorbed by CQDs, thus leading to the effective separation of photogenerated e--h+ pairs. The e- that migrate to the surface can react with the adsorbed O2 and reduce it to produce ·O2 -. In addition, the positive VB position allows h+ to oxidize OH- to generate ·OH. Both ·O2 - and ·OH can participate in the degradation reaction of BPA, but ·O2 - are the most critical active specie.

A novel B-doped CQDs/BMO photocatalyst was prepared by hydrothermal method for the degradation of BPA, and its structure, morphology, and photocatalytic properties were characterized in detail. SEM and TEM observations indicate that B-doped CQDs with a scale of less than 10 nm are dispersed on BMO nanometals, and the successful introduction of B element can be confirmed by FTIR and XPS measurements. The photodegradation results showed that the optimized BCQDs/BMO samples completely degraded BPA in 120 min under simulated sunlight, while the CQDs/BMO control samples without B doping only degraded approximately 84 % in 160 min. After filtering out wavelength components below 400 nm, the optimized combination of BCQDs/BMO showed no decrease in BPA degradation performance, indicating its strong visible light catalytic activity. The doping of B element improves the surface structure of the system, modulates the energy level of BMO, enhances the interface coupling with BMO through the introduction of a large number of functional groups, which is conducive to the absorption of visible light and provides more active sites. After four cycles, the samples still maintained high degradation performance. The current BCQDs/BMO catalysts simultaneously improve their catalytic performance in three important ways: active site increase, visible light absorption enhancement and carrier recombination inhibition, which will guide the design of other semiconductor-based photocatalysts.
